Weighted scoring for coding challenges — the math I didn't expect to care about
I started with a simple assumption: correctness is worth the most points. Two days later I had a spreadsheet full of edge cases.
Repos cleaned, portfolio live — what 'ready' actually looks like
After two weeks of prep work, the portfolio is live and the repos are presentable. Here's what that process actually involved.

import Callout from '../../components/Callout.astro';

There's a version of "getting ready to job search" that becomes a procrastination strategy. I've seen it. I almost became it. So I set a deadline: two weeks to get to a state I could call presentable, then start sending applications regardless of whether I felt ready.

This is what those two weeks looked like.

## The repo audit

I had 23 public repositories. Most were fine. Some were embarrassing. A few were actively wrong in ways that would raise flags.

What I dealt with:
- **Deleted 4 repos** — tutorial follow-alongs with no original work, one abandoned half-migration that had `TODO: fix this` in 6 places
- **Archived 7 repos** — real projects from 3+ years ago, not broken, just dated
- **Updated READMEs on 5 repos** — these are the ones I'd actually show someone. Added what the project does, how to run it, what I'd do differently now
- **Added a `.env.example`** to anything with environment variables — this should have been there from the start

<Callout type="note" label="Lesson">
The `.env.example` thing is embarrassing to admit. It's basic. But it's also the kind of thing that only matters when someone else is reading your code. I'd been writing for myself for a long time.
</Callout>

## The portfolio

I built a minimal portfolio page. Not this blog — a separate, intentionally stripped-down page that answers three questions: who I am, what I've built, how to reach me.

Stack: Astro + vanilla CSS. No React, no dependencies I don't need. The irony of a React developer building their portfolio in Astro is not lost on me. It builds to 4 files. It's fast. That's the point.

## What "ready" actually means

I told myself I'd know when I was ready. I don't think that's how it works.

What actually happened was I ran out of things that felt immediately broken. The portfolio was live. The READMEs were readable. The repos I'd point someone to were ones I wouldn't be embarrassed by.

That's not the same as ready. That's "defensible." And defensible is enough to start.

<Callout type="warn" label="What I'd skip">
I spent almost a full day restyling a project from 2021 to use my current CSS patterns. Completely unnecessary. Nobody looking at a 4-year-old project expects it to use 2025 conventions. Sunk cost thinking. Don't do this.
</Callout>

Week four is applications. The prep phase is done.
There's a version of kindness that's really just conflict avoidance wearing a friendly face. I practiced it for years.
what I learned from never saying the hard thing

A student had been stuck on the same concept for three weeks. I kept finding gentler ways to re-explain it. I told myself I was being patient. I was actually just avoiding the conversation that needed to happen.
There's a version of kindness that's really just conflict avoidance wearing a friendly face. I practiced it for years. In a classroom it costs a student weeks. On a dev team it costs everyone more than that. ## The situation Teaching beginner developers means working with people who are often frightened of being wrong. They've chosen a hard thing and they're not sure they can do it. The instinct — my instinct — was to soften every correction, find the positive angle, never say "that approach won't work" without immediately following it with five reasons they should feel good about themselves. It felt like good pedagogy. It felt like psychological safety. What it actually was: a way of never giving anyone the direct signal they needed to actually change. ## What I believed at the time I thought ambiguity was mercy. That if I said "this is an interesting approach, and another way to think about it might be..." I was respecting their process. I wasn't. I was making them decode my feedback instead of just receiving it. I was optimizing for the feeling of the conversation instead of its outcome. The hard truth is that unclear feedback from a person in authority — a teacher, a tech lead, a senior dev in code review — creates more anxiety than direct feedback. Ambiguity doesn't feel safe. It feels like a trap you don't know how to avoid.
the lesson
What Broke
Softening the message erased the message
A student who needed to hear "you're not practicing enough outside class" heard "you're doing great, just keep going." They kept going the same way for another month. That's on me. The conversation I avoided cost them four weeks they couldn't get back. I thought I was being kind. I was being comfortable.
What Held
Direct doesn't mean cold — it means clear
When I finally started saying the actual thing — "this solution is too slow for production, here's specifically why" — the room didn't collapse. People leaned in. They knew where they stood. A developer who knows exactly what isn't working yet can fix it. One who only receives soft signals can't. Clarity turned out to be the more respectful choice.
## The shift The reframe that actually worked: feedback isn't about how it feels to give it. It's about whether the other person can act on it. If they can't, it wasn't feedback — it was performance. On a dev team this shows up in code review, in sprint retros, in the moment someone's architecture decision is going to cause pain in six weeks and you know it. The lead — formal or not — is the person who names the thing, clearly and early, before the cost compounds. That's not a management skill. It's a communication skill. It's something you can practice at any level of a team, without a title, starting now.
What's the most recent piece of feedback you softened so much it stopped being useful? What would you say if you said it plainly?
--- *Hard Won is a series about dev leadership without the title — lessons from a decade of teaching, and what they cost to learn.*
Week three: two rejections, one callback, and what I learned from all three
No sugarcoating. Two rejections arrived in one afternoon. The callback arrived the next morning. Here's what I did differently.

import Callout from '../../components/Callout.astro';

Week three of the job search looked like this: two rejection emails arrived on the same afternoon. The callback arrived the next morning. I'm going to write about all three, because the pattern matters more than the outcome.

## The rejections

Both rejections were for senior frontend roles. Both came after a first-round technical screen. Neither gave specific feedback, which is both frustrating and completely expected.

What I can reconstruct from memory:

- **Company A** — I overcomplicated the component architecture question. They asked for a simple data table. I started talking about virtualization before I'd even confirmed requirements. Classic trap.
- **Company B** — The take-home was fine. The system design conversation after it wasn't. I hadn't thought through the caching layer at all, and it showed.

<Callout type="note" label="Pattern">
In both cases I defaulted to "show what I know" instead of "solve what they're asking." That's a teaching reflex. It needs to be coached out for interviews.
</Callout>

## The callback

The callback came from a team I'd described as a long-shot. Smaller company, interesting problem domain, full-stack role. I was more relaxed going into the screen because I expected less. I actually listened to what they asked instead of pattern-matching to what I'd rehearsed.

The question that went well: they asked how I'd approach debugging a production performance issue. I walked through it like I was thinking out loud — what I'd instrument first, what I'd rule out, where I'd look for unexpected bottlenecks. No canned answer. Just process.

## What I'm changing

1. **Lead with clarifying questions.** Every technical question gets at least one clarifying question before I start solving.
2. **Scope before architecture.** Define what "done" looks like before proposing how to build it.
3. **Practice thinking out loud.** Not explaining what I know — narrating what I'm doing as I do it.

<Callout type="warn" label="Honest take">
The gap between "I know how to build this" and "I can demonstrate that I know how to build this under interview conditions" is real and larger than I expected after a decade away from job searching.
</Callout>

The search continues. Week four starts Monday.
